meta.scene_summary: We are busy building the future on top of infrastructure that is currently screaming for help.

cooking.headline: Chevron is now basically a cloud provider's personal extension cord
cooking.summary: Chevron just signed a 20-year deal to power Microsoft's data centers, effectively pivoting from oil tycoon to digital landlord. It's nice to know that your future cloud compute bills will be directly tied to the price of a barrel of crude.
cooking.plain_english: Chevron has entered a long-term agreement to supply electricity to Microsoft for its data center operations in West Texas.

cooking.headline: Tech workers are finally hitting the 'no' button on the AI hype train
cooking.summary: Employees are increasingly pushing back against the frantic AI pivot, proving that even the people building the tools are tired of the feature creep. It is the corporate equivalent of realizing your boss's 'urgent' project is actually just a vanity metric that nobody asked for.
cooking.plain_english: Technology employees are organizing to protest and challenge the rapid, industry-wide focus on artificial intelligence development.

cooking.headline: Meta's training data collection is the new 'please don't touch my stuff'
cooking.summary: Meta is effectively asking employees to hand over their work as the secret sauce for future AI models, and people are finally drawing a line in the sand. It is the corporate equivalent of your landlord asking for the keys to your apartment so they can rent out your bedroom while you're at work.
cooking.plain_english: Employees have started a petition to object to Meta using their internal work data to train artificial intelligence models.

cooking.headline: Another day, another 'sovereign' AI foundation enters the chat
cooking.summary: Everyone is launching their own open foundation model to avoid relying on Big Tech, as if the AI landscape isn't already a crowded group project. It's the digital equivalent of every friend group insisting they need their own private Discord server instead of just using the main one.
cooking.plain_english: Apertus has released an open-source artificial intelligence model designed for independent organizations to use without relying on major technology companies.

cooking.headline: Mitchell Hashimoto is single-handedly funding the Zig economy
cooking.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto just dropped another $400k on the Zig Software Foundation, proving that if you like a tool enough, you eventually just start paying its rent. It's like being the only person in a group project who actually enjoys the work and decides to buy snacks for the whole team.
cooking.plain_english: Software developer Mitchell Hashimoto has donated $400,000 to the organization that manages the development of the Zig programming language.

cooking.headline: Deno wants to live on your desktop, because the browser isn't enough
cooking.summary: Deno is launching a desktop runtime to let you build apps that run locally with all the modern trimmings. It's like they looked at Electron and decided to fix the 'my RAM is crying' problem once and for all.
cooking.plain_english: Deno has introduced a new way for developers to create desktop applications using their software environment.

cooked.headline: The Canadian government spent $46M on Palantir without telling anyone
cooked.summary: Canada funneled nearly $47 million into a secret Palantir contract, proving that transparency is just a suggestion when you have enough data to crunch. It's like finding a massive recurring subscription charge on your company card that nobody remembers approving.
cooked.plain_english: The Canadian government allocated $46.8 million for a confidential contract with the data analytics company Palantir.

cooked.headline: The NSF is trading long-term discovery for short-term hype cycles
cooked.summary: The government is gutting basic research budgets to fund the latest tech trends, because why fund the next foundation of physics when you can subsidize a shiny new chatbot? It is like selling your house to buy a lottery ticket because you heard someone else won big.
cooked.plain_english: The National Science Foundation is reallocating money from fundamental scientific research programs to support new technology initiatives.

cooked.headline: Codex is speedrunning the destruction of your SSD
cooked.summary: OpenAI's logging bug is currently writing terabytes of data to your local drive like it's trying to win a storage-filling contest. It turns out the most dangerous thing your AI assistant does isn't hallucinating code, but silently murdering your hardware.
cooked.plain_english: A software bug in OpenAI's Codex tool is causing it to save excessive amounts of data, which can prematurely wear out computer storage drives.

cooked.headline: UUIDv7 is now just a static number in a trench coat
cooked.summary: Golang's UUIDv7 implementation on browsers is currently hardcoded to 7000, making every generated ID identical. It's essentially the software equivalent of a group project where everyone just copies the same wrong answer.
cooked.plain_english: A technical error in the Go programming language causes it to generate the same identification number repeatedly when used in web browsers.
